# Executive Summary {-}

This report presents a governance and prioritization framework for restoration of the Neexdzii Kwah (Upper Bulkley River) watershed, grounded in Wet'suwet'en stewardship values and supported by an integrated suite of open-source spatial analysis tools. The framework is not a static plan — it is a living system designed to evolve as understanding deepens and new data becomes available.

<br>

**Governance and prioritization.** The prioritization framework proposes a four-tier governance structure — Stewardship Council, Technical Working Group, Implementation Partnerships, and Community Roundtable — that places Wet'suwet'en direction at the centre of restoration decision-making. Three diagnostic gates filter candidate sites before investment: certainty of diagnosis, whether active degradation is ongoing, and access and willingness to act. Scoring principles were developed from two community workshops — one with Wet'suwet'en members and leadership, one with local landowners — where participants ranked restoration parameters. Wet'suwet'en ranked Cultural Significance highest; landowners ranked Watershed Function highest. Both perspectives are built into the weighting system. The framework is honest that habitat restoration alone cannot deliver salmon recovery — Skeena chinook and coho show sustained declines consistent with exploitation rates exceeding what these populations can sustain — but rebuilding habitat capacity is the prerequisite for recovery when exploitation is eventually reduced.

<br>

**Sub-basin delineation.** The watershed was divided into 14 sub-basins to enable prioritization and facilitate communication of issues and opportunities within each area. Natural break points — Bulkley Falls and Buck Falls — define the primary divisions due to their significance as barriers to salmon access. Within these regions, sub-basins were delineated based on groupings of habitat and landscape characteristics at comparable scales, using the BC Freshwater Atlas stream network and interactive watershed delineation tools. Each sub-basin integrates fish habitat modelling, land ownership, reserve lands, cultural sites, and land cover change into a single characterization that supports comparison and investment targeting.

<br>

**Floodplain modelling and land cover change.** The functional floodplain — where the river does geomorphic work including sediment storage, nutrient exchange, riparian recruitment, and large woody debris delivery — was modelled using the Valley Confinement Algorithm. Within this extent, satellite-derived land cover classification (2017–2023) reveals a consistent pattern of tree cover loss and agriculture expansion. The magnitude of detected change depends on the stream network used to define the floodplain model: using 4th order and larger streams yields approximately 680 ha of net tree cover loss, while expanding to 3rd order coho habitat streams anchored to 1st order tributaries increases the estimate to over 1,000 ha. Both are valid representations at different scales — the range reflects real sensitivity to model assumptions and underscores why documenting those assumptions matters. Individual sub-basin tree loss rates range from -1% to -22%, with agriculture expansion up to 59% in the most affected areas. Ongoing work will further quantify these scenarios to visualize the different stream networks, resulting floodplain extents, and land cover outcomes.

<br>

**Historic aerial photographs.** Nearly 10,000 georeferenced provincial aerial photographs (1963–2019) have been processed and served as a queryable spatial catalog using the same infrastructure as the UAV imagery. This 60-year visual record allows direct comparison of historic and current conditions at any location in the watershed — grounding restoration targets in documented prior condition rather than modelled estimates alone.

<br>

**Site assessments.** Field reviews at 26 locations over 2024–2025 documented conditions at past restoration sites, historic prescription locations, and newly proposed areas. A recurring theme was riparian and floodplain vegetation removal and cattle-related degradation at scales far exceeding the footprint of past restoration investments. At sites where work has been completed, the widths set aside for riparian recovery were insufficient. Road and rail erosion protection consistently lacked vegetated riprap, soft armouring, and effective riparian buffers. UAV flights at the majority of sites provide high-resolution baseline imagery for future monitoring.

<br>

**Aquatic health monitoring.** Benthic invertebrate sampling was conducted at three sites on the Neexdzii Kwah mainstem within high-value chinook spawning and rearing habitat, with triplicate kick samples at each site to enable statistical distinction between real site differences and natural variability. The report on taxonomic analysis results is near completion. Environmental DNA (eDNA) sampling was conducted below Bulkley Falls and approximately 1 km downstream.

<br>

**Tools as deliverables.** The spatial analysis tools developed for this work — floodplain delineation (`flooded`), land cover change detection (`drift`), airphoto georeferencing (`fly`), sub-basin delineation (`fresh`), and the STAC imagery infrastructure — are open-source R packages designed to work at any scale and in any watershed in the province. They are not single-use products tied to this project. The capacity they build — for ourselves and for others — to understand and manage conservation and restoration is as much a deliverable as the analysis results themselves.

<br>

This report is a living document, with ongoing revisions and updates tracked in the [project changelog](https://github.com/NewGraphEnvironment/restoration_wedzin_kwa_2024/blob/main/NEWS.md). We are humbled by the depth of knowledge that exists for this region, both through traditional knowledge and scientific study. Recovery efforts must go beyond technical interventions and include a commitment to learning from the vast body of knowledge held by the Wet'suwet'en people — including cultural practices, oral tradition (Kungax), Wet'suwet'en Law (Ink Nu'at'en), and the deep connections between families, house groups, and the lands and waters that sustain them [@harris2011Yinkadinii; @universityofbritishcolumbialibraryDelgamuukwTrial; @morin2016NiwhtsideniHibiiten]. We acknowledge that this report is far from complete, and that the work of restoring and sustaining ecosystem values will require many decades — if not centuries — of dedication. It is our hope that this effort can serve as a catalyst for positive change and foster genuine, open collaboration.
